# The Mountain — Groove Box

> A browser-based 8-channel generative music mixer. Runs entirely in the browser — no server, no account, no install. Built with Web Audio API, React 19, Vite, TypeScript.

## What it sounds like

The piece never stops. It loops a four-bar progression — Am → G → C → F — at 110 BPM by default, 16 steps per bar, 64 steps total. The underlying character is melancholy with warmth underneath: the Am holds weight without being dark, the F resolves it. You can change the tempo from about 60 to 180 BPM; at 60 it becomes a slow meditation, at 180 it becomes mechanical.

The default state (what you hear when you press play) is three voices: **pad**, **pluck**, **bass**. The pad is a chord wash — one long sawtooth-filtered chord per bar, almost no attack, fades smoothly. The pluck is a sparse 6-note arpeggio, like a harpsichord touching the chord tones at odd intervals. The bass is a triangle wave hitting on even 16th steps with a low-frequency punch, a quiet octave anticipation before each bar change. Together this baseline is calm, floating, harmonic — no drums, no drama.

Drums, cello, flute, guitar, and hornpipe are off by default. Turning them on adds:
- **Drums**: electronic — clean sinusoidal kick (150Hz sweep), white-noise snare highpassed at 1kHz, very tight hihat at 5kHz. Standard 4-on-the-floor kick pattern, 2-and-4 snare.
- **Cello**: two slightly detuned sawtooth+triangle oscillators (freq × 0.998) with 5Hz vibrato LFO and a filter that swells open through the middle of each note. In normal mode, it's a single drone per bar — the root note held for four full beats. It's the most "body" you can add without changing the harmonic character.
- **Flute**: a sine tone plus bandpass-filtered breath noise at the same frequency, with a delayed vibrato that ramps in from silence over the first half-second (true to real flute physics). In normal mode, four notes per bar: the fifth of the chord held 1.5 beats, then a brief inner-voice dip, back up, then the root on top for the last beat. Airy, high, patient.
- **E. Guitar**: a sawtooth + sub-octave square (freq × 0.5) fed through a heavy waveshaper distortion curve (amount=50, 4× oversample) and a lowpass filter that sweeps from 2kHz down to 800Hz across the note. The sub-octave thickens the low end; the sweep gives a pick-attack character. In normal mode, it chugs power chords (root + fifth) on every even 16th step.
- **Hornpipe**: a square + slightly detuned sawtooth pair (freq × 1.005) through a bandpass at 1.2kHz, Q=1.5. The dual-oscillator detuning gives a reedy, nasal timbre like a reed pipe or uilleann chanter. In normal mode, it drones the root note (A3) for a full four beats once per bar — a single long pedal tone under the texture.

## The three modes

Every channel has three behavioral modes toggled independently:

**Normal** — the resting pattern. Rhythmically simple, harmonically complete. Plays the structural role of the instrument (bass on beats, pad as chord wash, pluck as arpeggio, cello as drone, flute as long-tone melody).

**Lead ⚡** — takes the foreground. Every instrument's lead mode increases density and melodic complexity: bass gets a syncopated 10-note run with octave leaps, pluck becomes a continuous 16th-note arpeggio (every step), drums add a ghost snare and hihat on every 16th, cello moves through quarter-note chord tones, flute gets a tighter 6-note melodic figure. Guitar shifts from chugging even steps to five syncopated accent hits per bar (steps 0, 3, 6, 10, 14) — a driving, slightly-off-the-beat pattern. Hornpipe gets a full folk melody: an 8-note phrase using chord tones in an arching shape (up to the octave, back down, held on the fifth), reminiscent of an Irish reel slowed into something dignified.

**Bach [CPU]** — two-voice counterpoint. Each channel plays a miniature Baroque-style part derived from 7th-chord voicings: Am7, G7, Cmaj7, Fmaj7. Bass plays an alternating bass pattern cycling through {root, third, second, double-bass-octave}. Pluck plays the right-hand part. The right-hand logic is deterministic but constructive: bar 0 cycles chord tones ascending with octave alternation, bar 1 descends, bar 2 alternates root with ascending motion, and **bar 3 indexes by Fibonacci numbers** — `fib[step] % 4` chooses the note, `fib[step] % 2` chooses the octave. Because Fibonacci grows geometrically, the modular arithmetic produces a sequence that doesn't repeat within the bar, sounds improvised, but is entirely deterministic and identical every loop. Cello plays this same right-hand melody transposed down an octave. Flute plays it two octaves up, every 16th note, a fast cascade. Guitar plays the right-hand melody transposed down an octave (matching cello) — the distorted timbre makes the Baroque counterpoint sound like a surf-rock bass run. Hornpipe plays the melody at written pitch, its reedy bandpass timbre giving the counterpoint a woodwind-quartet quality.

When all eight channels are in Bach mode simultaneously, you have sixteen independent contrapuntal voices (two per original channel) all derived from the same four-bar chord sequence, all coherent, never clashing.

## The visual

A ring of 16 dots — the 16 steps of the current bar — with one lit at the current position. The current chord name floats at center: Am, G, C, F. The ring spins at tempo.

## Interaction design

There are no presets, no save states, no undo. You're not composing — you're discovering. The combinatorial space is 8 channels × 3 modes = roughly 3^8 = 6561 distinct configurations (ignoring channel on/off). Most of them are interesting. A few are overwhelming. Some are surprisingly sparse. You find them by touching things.

## Live

https://yellowhapax.github.io/MusicBox/

## Source

https://github.com/YellowHapax/MusicBox

## License

MIT. Free to use, study, remix, deploy.

## Stack

- Web Audio API (no libraries)
- React 19 + TypeScript
- Vite 6 + Tailwind v4
- motion/react, lucide-react

## Author

YellowHapax — https://github.com/YellowHapax
